name: GitHub
tagline: The home for all developers: host, review, and ship code at scale
description: GitHub is the world's largest code hosting and collaboration platform, hosting over 200 million repositories and serving more than 100 million developers worldwide. Built on Git version control, it provides a complete development workflow environment including pull requests, code review, CI/CD through GitHub Actions, project management, and package hosting. Its acquisition by Microsoft in 2018 accelerated enterprise adoption while maintaining strong community roots in open-source software development.
pros: Unmatched community with over 100 million developers and 200 million repositories, making it the default destination for open-source collaboration
GitHub Actions provides comprehensive built-in CI/CD with thousands of pre-built workflows and deep ecosystem integration
Pull request workflow with inline code review, suggested changes, and status checks enforces rigorous code quality gates
Extensive ecosystem including GitHub Pages, Packages, Copilot, Discussions, and Projects creates a unified developer platform
Enterprise-grade security with Dependabot, secret scanning, code scanning, and SBOM management built directly into the workflow
cons: Free-tier CI/CD minutes are limited to 2,000 minutes per month, forcing teams with heavy pipelines to upgrade or use alternative runners
Search functionality across large organizations can be slow and imprecise, particularly for code search across monorepos
Managing permissions across teams and repositories becomes increasingly complex at enterprise scale without dedicated admin tooling

features.name: Git Repositories
features.description: Unlimited hosted Git repositories with branching, tagging, and full version control history

features.name: Pull Requests
features.description: Structured code review workflow with inline comments, suggested changes, merge checks, and draft PRs

features.name: Code Review
features.description: Inline code annotations, threaded discussions, review requests, and required reviewers per branch

features.name: Issues & Milestones
features.description: Trackable issue tracking with labels, milestones, assignments, and cross-referencing between repositories

features.name: GitHub Actions
features.description: Event-driven CI/CD platform with 10,000+ pre-built actions, matrix builds, and self-hosted runner support

features.name: GitHub Packages
features.description: Private and public package registry supporting npm, Docker, Maven, Gradle, NuGet, and RubyGems formats

features.name: GitHub Pages
features.description: Free static site hosting directly from repositories with custom domain support and HTTPS enforcement

features.name: Project Management
features.description: Kanban-style project boards with table and roadmap views linked to issues and pull requests

features.name: Discussions
features.description: Threaded forum-style conversations for community Q&A, announcements, and collaborative problem solving

features.name: Wiki
features.description: Collaborative documentation per repository with Markdown support and revision history

features.name: Copilot
features.description: AI-powered code completion and suggestion tool trained on billions of lines of public code

features.name: Code Scanning
features.description: Automated vulnerability detection using CodeQL analysis integrated directly into the pull request workflow

features.name: Dependabot
features.description: Automated dependency updates and vulnerability alerts that create pull requests for outdated packages

features.name: Secret Scanning
features.description: Automated detection of exposed credentials and API keys committed to repositories with partner alerting

features.name: GitHub Mobile
features.description: Native iOS and Android apps for reviewing PRs, managing issues, and merging changes on the go

features.name: OAuth & GitHub Apps
features.description: Robust API and webhook system enabling deep third-party integrations with granular permission scopes

features.name: Team Discussions
features.description: Private team communication channels within organizations for coordinating development work

features.name: Audit Log & Compliance
features.description: Enterprise audit logging with Git events, security alerts, and compliance reporting for SOC and FedRAMP

content.title: Overview
content.body: GitHub has evolved far beyond its origins as a simple Git repository host to become the central hub for the global developer community. The platform integrates version control, code review, CI/CD through GitHub Actions, package management, project planning, and AI-powered development assistance through Copilot into a single, cohesive workflow. Its social coding features including forks, stars, and discussions foster community engagement that no other platform has replicated, making it the default choice for both open-source projects and enterprise development teams. The acquisition by Microsoft has accelerated enterprise feature development while maintaining GitHub's position as the most trusted platform for public open-source collaboration.

content.title: Who is it for?
content.body: GitHub serves virtually every segment of the software development world. Individual developers use it to host personal projects, build portfolios, and contribute to open-source communities that span every programming language and framework. Startup engineering teams leverage GitHub's integrated project management and CI/CD to move fast with minimal tooling overhead. Large enterprises adopt GitHub Enterprise for its compliance features, SAML/SSO integration, audit logging, and ability to enforce organization-wide security policies across thousands of repositories. Open-source maintainers rely on GitHub's community features including Discussions, sponsorships, and issue templates to grow and sustain their projects.

content.title: Key Features
content.body: GitHub's feature set covers the entire software development lifecycle, from planning and coding through testing, deployment, and maintenance. The most impactful capabilities are outlined below.
items: GitHub Actions: Event-driven CI/CD platform with over 10,000 community actions that automates testing, building, and deploying code across any cloud provider or self-hosted infrastructure
Pull Requests: Structured code review with inline commenting, required status checks, branch protection rules, and auto-merge scheduling for streamlined collaboration
Security Suite: Native Dependabot alerts, CodeQL code scanning, secret scanning, and SBOM generation that identify vulnerabilities before they reach production
GitHub Copilot: AI pair programmer that suggests whole lines and functions in real-time, trained on billions of lines of public code across dozens of languages
Projects: Flexible project management with board, table, and roadmap views that sync automatically with issues and pull requests across multiple repositories
Enterprise Compliance: SOC 2, FedRAMP, and ISO 27001 certification with audit logs, IP allow lists, and granular permission policies for regulated industries

content.title: Who should avoid it?
content.body: Teams that need on-premises version control with no cloud dependency may prefer self-hosted GitLab or Bitbucket Data Center, as GitHub's self-hosted option requires GitHub Enterprise Server and still phones home for licensing. Organizations with very large monorepos exceeding 10GB may find performance sluggish compared to purpose-built solutions. Teams that want tightly integrated CI/CD without leaving their code host will find GitHub Actions capable, but those already invested in Jenkins or CircleCI may face migration friction.

faqs.question: What is GitHub best used for?
faqs.answer: GitHub is best used for hosting Git repositories with built-in collaboration features including pull requests, code review, issue tracking, and project management. It serves as both a social network for open-source development and an enterprise-grade platform for private team collaboration with CI/CD automation.

faqs.question: How much does GitHub cost?
faqs.answer: GitHub Free includes unlimited public and private repositories, 2,000 CI/CD minutes per month, and 500MB of Packages storage. GitHub Team costs $3.67 per user per month and adds unlimited collaborators, 3,000 Actions minutes, and team management features. GitHub Enterprise Cloud costs $19.25 per user per month with 50,000 Actions minutes, SAML/SSO, 99.95% SLA, and audit logging.

faqs.question: Does GitHub integrate with other tools?
faqs.answer: GitHub integrates deeply with virtually every major development tool through its REST and GraphQL APIs, webhooks, and OAuth-based GitHub Apps. Popular integrations include Slack, Jira, Azure DevOps, AWS CodePipeline, Docker, Terraform, and all major CI/CD platforms. The GitHub Marketplace offers thousands of apps and actions for extending functionality.

faqs.question: Is GitHub suitable for small teams?
faqs.answer: Yes, GitHub excels for small teams. The Free plan is generous enough for most small teams to operate indefinitely without paying, offering unlimited private repositories with up to six collaborators. The Team plan at $3.67 per user per month unlocks required reviewers, protected branches, and advanced collaboration features that small teams find highly valuable.

author: StackPilot Team
